questions.title: Missing Null Terminator
questions.description: What happens when this code runs?
options: Prints ABC followed by garbage characters
questions.explanation: The array has no null terminator. When printf tries to print it as a string (%s), it will continue reading past the array until it finds a '\0', potentially printing garbage. Fix: Use char str[4] = {'A', 'B', 'C', '\0'}; or char str[] = "ABC";.
hints: Remember that strings in C must end with '\0'
Remember that the array has no null terminator
%s expects a null-terminated string
questions.hint: Remember that C strings are null-terminated arrays of characters.

questions.title: String Length vs Size
questions.explanation: Strlen(str) counts characters until '\0', giving 5. sizeof(str) gives the total array size including the null terminator, giving 6. Remember: sizeof includes the '\0', strlen doesn't count it. sizeof returns the size in bytes at compile time. For arrays, it only works on the original array, not a pointer parameter.

Think about sizeof counts total bytes in the array

questions.hint: Think about what sizeof returns - the size in bytes, not the number of elements.

questions.title: Safe String Copy
questions.description: Which is the SAFEST way to copy a string with size limit?

Remember that always null-terminate the destination

questions.title: Modifying String Literal
questions.explanation: Attempting to modify a string literal is undefined behavior. String literals are typically stored in read-only memory. questions.title: strncpy Padding Behavior
questions.explanation: Strncpy() copies up to n characters. If the source is shorter than n, it pads the remaining space with null bytes. Here, 'Hi' (2 chars + 1 null = 3 bytes) is copied, then strncpy pads with 2 more null bytes to reach 5 total. The remaining 'XXXXX' stays. However, the string prints as 'Hi' because printf stops at the first null terminator.

questions.hint: Think about: "hi" (2 chars) + "abc" (3 chars) + '\0' requires 6 bytes.

questions.title: strrchr Last Occurrence
questions.explanation: Strrchr() returns a pointer to the last occurrence of 'g' in the string. In 'programming', the last 'g' is at index 10. ptr - str gives the offset from the start of the string, which is 10. Pointer arithmetic in C moves the pointer by multiples of the pointed-to type's size, not by bytes.
hints: Consider strrchr finds the last occurrence
Remember that count from the beginning to find the index
Remember that the string is 'programming'

questions.title: strstr Substring Search
questions.explanation: Strstr() returns a pointer to the first occurrence of the substring 'quick' in str. It finds 'quick' starting at index 4 and returns a pointer to that position. Printing from that pointer prints 'quick brown fox'.
hints: Consider strstr finds the first occurrence of a substring
Remember that it returns a pointer to where the substring starts
Remember that what comes after 'quick' in the string?

questions.title: strstr Not Found
questions.explanation: Strstr() returns NULL if the substring is not found in the string. Since 'world' is not present in 'Hello', strstr returns NULL and 'Not found' is printed.

Remember that check if 'world' exists in 'Hello'
Remember that nULL indicates failure

questions.title: strtok Basic Tokenization
questions.explanation: Strtok() breaks a string into tokens based on delimiters. The first call returns a pointer to the first token 'one'. It replaces the delimiter ',' with a null terminator internally.
hints: Consider strtok splits the string by delimiters
Remember that the first call returns the first token
The delimiter is ','

questions.title: strtok Multiple Calls
questions.explanation: The first strtok call returns 'red'. Subsequent calls with NULL as the first argument continue tokenizing the same string. The second call returns 'green'.

Remember that the first call returns 'red'
Remember that the second call returns the next token

questions.title: strtok Modifies String
questions.explanation: Strtok() modifies the original string by replacing delimiters with null terminators. After the first call, the first '-' is replaced with '\0', so printing str prints only 'a' (up to the null terminator).
hints: Consider strtok modifies the original string
Remember that it replaces delimiters with null terminators
The solution involves printf stops at the first null terminator
questions.hint: Remember that C strings are null-terminated arrays of characters.

questions.title: sprintf Basic Formatting
questions.explanation: Sprintf() formats and stores a string in a buffer instead of printing to stdout. It works like printf but writes to the buffer. Here it formats 'The answer is 42' into the buffer.
hints: Consider sprintf writes formatted output to a string
Remember that it works like printf but to a buffer
%d is replaced with the value of num
questions.hint: Think about: sprintf() formats and stores a string in a buffer instead of printing to stdout.

questions.title: snprintf Buffer Safety
questions.explanation: Snprintf() writes at most n-1 characters to the buffer (leaving room for null terminator). With a 10-byte buffer, it writes 'Hello Wor' (9 chars + null). However, it returns 12, the length of the string that would have been written if the buffer were large enough.
hints: Consider snprintf truncates output to fit the buffer
Remember that it returns the length that would have been written
Remember that the buffer can hold 9 characters plus null terminator
questions.hint: Remember that C strings are null-terminated arrays of characters.

questions.title: String Literal vs Array
questions.description: What is the difference between these declarations?
options: str1 can be modified, str2 cannot
str2 can be modified, str1 cannot
questions.explanation: Str1 is a pointer to a string literal stored in read-only memory and should not be modified (undefined behavior). str2 is a character array initialized with a copy of the string, stored on the stack, and can be modified.
hints: Remember that string literals are stored in read-only memory
Remember that arrays store their own copy of data
Remember that modifying string literals is undefined behavior
questions.hint: Remember that this code exhibits undefined behavior. Consider what C standard says about this operation.

questions.title: String Literal Modification
questions.description: What happens when this program runs?
questions.explanation: String literals are stored in read-only memory. Attempting to modify a string literal through a pointer results in undefined behavior, which may cause a segmentation fault or other runtime errors. Undefined behavior means the C standard doesn't specify what should happen. Avoid it by following language rules carefully.
hints: Remember that string literals are read-only
Remember that modifying read-only memory is undefined behavior
Remember that this may crash at runtime
questions.hint: Remember that this code exhibits undefined behavior. Consider what C standard says about this operation.

questions.title: Buffer Overflow Detection
questions.description: What is the problem with this code?
questions.explanation: The buffer has space for 5 characters, but 'Hello' requires 6 bytes (5 characters + null terminator). strcpy will write beyond the buffer boundaries, causing a buffer overflow and undefined behavior. Undefined behavior means the C standard doesn't specify what should happen. Avoid it by following language rules carefully.
hints: Remember that count the bytes needed including null terminator
Think about strcpy doesn't check buffer size
Remember that writing beyond buffer boundaries is dangerous
questions.hint: Remember that this code exhibits undefined behavior. Consider what C standard says about this operation.

questions.title: Array Bounds Safety
questions.explanation: Sizeof() returns the total size of the array in bytes, not the string length. The array is declared with size 6, so sizeof(str) returns 6, regardless of the content. sizeof returns the size in bytes at compile time. For arrays, it only works on the original array, not a pointer parameter.
hints: Consider sizeof returns the size of the array, not string length
Remember that the array size is specified as 6
sizeof is compile-time, not runtime
questions.hint: Think about what sizeof returns - the size in bytes, not the number of elements.

questions.title: strlen vs sizeof
questions.explanation: Strlen() returns the number of characters before the null terminator (4 for 'Test'). sizeof() returns the total allocated size of the array (10 bytes). sizeof returns the size in bytes at compile time. For arrays, it only works on the original array, not a pointer parameter.
hints: Consider strlen counts characters until null terminator
Think about sizeof returns total array size
Remember that array was declared with size 10
questions.hint: Think about what sizeof returns - the size in bytes, not the number of elements.

questions.title: strncpy No Null Terminator
questions.description: What is the potential issue with this code?
questions.explanation: Strncpy copies exactly 5 bytes from src to dest. Since 'Hello' is 5 characters, there's no room for the null terminator in the 5 bytes copied. dest is not null-terminated, causing undefined behavior when printf tries to print it.
hints: Consider strncpy doesn't guarantee null termination
Think about src has exactly 5 characters plus null terminator
Remember that only 5 bytes are copied
questions.hint: Remember that this code exhibits undefined behavior. Consider what C standard says about this operation.

questions.title: strtok Edge Case
questions.explanation: Strtok() treats consecutive delimiters as a single delimiter and skips them. It only returns non-empty tokens. Leading and trailing delimiters are also ignored. So it prints 'a b'.
hints: Consider strtok skips empty tokens
Remember that consecutive delimiters are treated as one
Remember that leading/trailing delimiters are ignored
questions.hint: Think about: strtok() treats consecutive delimiters as a single delimiter and skips them.

questions.title: sprintf Return Value
questions.explanation: Sprintf() returns the number of characters written to the buffer (excluding the null terminator). 'Count: 123' has 10 characters, so sprintf returns 10.
hints: Consider sprintf returns the number of characters written
Remember that count the characters in 'Count: 123'
Remember that the null terminator is not counted
questions.hint: Think about: sprintf() returns the number of characters written to the buffer (excluding the null terminator).

questions.title: strchr Null Character
questions.explanation: Strchr() can find the null terminator. Every C string ends with '\0', so strchr(str, '\0') returns a pointer to the null terminator, not NULL. Therefore, 'Found' is printed.
hints: Consider strchr can search for the null terminator
Remember that all C strings end with '\0'
Remember that it returns a pointer to the terminator, not NULL
questions.hint: Think about what happens when you dereference a NULL pointer.

questions.title: strcat Buffer Overflow Risk
questions.description: What is wrong with this code?
questions.explanation: Dest has 10 bytes. 'Hello' takes 6 bytes (including null). ' World' takes 7 bytes (including null). Together they need 12 bytes (5+6+1), exceeding the 10-byte buffer. strcat causes a buffer overflow. Undefined behavior means the C standard doesn't specify what should happen. Avoid it by following language rules carefully.
hints: Remember that count total bytes needed including null terminator
Think about dest has only 10 bytes
strcat doesn't check buffer size

questions.title: strncat Safe Concatenation
questions.explanation: Dest has 10 bytes, 'Hi' uses 3 (including null). Remaining space is 7 bytes. We calculate sizeof(dest) - strlen(dest) - 1 = 10 - 2 - 1 = 7. strncat appends at most 7 chars, but always null-terminates. So it appends 'Hi' + '123456' (6 chars) to fit in 9 total + null.
hints: Remember that calculate available space correctly
Think about strncat always adds null terminator
Remember that account for existing content and null byte
questions.hint: Think about what sizeof returns - the size in bytes, not the number of elements.

questions.title: strcmp vs strncmp
questions.explanation: Strcmp compares the entire strings and finds them different ('test' vs 'testing'), returning non-zero (r1 == 0 is false, prints 0). strncmp compares only the first 4 characters ('test' vs 'test'), which match, returning 0 (r2 == 0 is true, prints 1).
hints: Consider strcmp compares entire strings
Think about strncmp compares only n characters
Remember that first 4 characters are the same
